text:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

## Context management (automatic)

To keep your context small and your runs cheap, the harness automatically
elides the *raw text* of large `read`/`bash` outputs after they have been on
screen for a few turns, replacing each with a short `[context-saver: …]` stub
that names the file or command. The document was fully read when it was
current — nothing was hidden from your initial pass — only the bulk is dropped
from later turns. Two habits keep this lossless:

- As you read a document, **record the specific facts, figures, dates, and
  verbatim clauses you will need** into your notes or draft. Don't rely on the
  full text staying resident.
- If you later need the exact text of a doc that now shows a `[context-saver:
  …]` stub, simply **`read` that file again** — the full content returns.

## File-format skills

The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
formats. Read them before tackling the task.


## Skill: docx

---
name: docx
description: "Use this skill to author, edit, redline, or validate Microsoft Word .docx files. Covers creating new documents from markdown or templates, editing existing documents in place, generating tracked-changes redlines, adding comments, and accepting/rejecting revisions. For READING existing .docx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'draft a memo', 'mark up the agreement', 'redline this', 'add comments to', 'fill the engagement letter template'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .xlsx, .pptx, or .doc (legacy Word)."
---

# DOCX authoring, editing, redlining

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .docx, use the harness `read` tool. It already returns structured text via pandoc. This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *validating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a new doc from markdown | `scripts/generate_from_md.py` (Pandoc + reference template) |
| Generate a new doc programmatically | `python-docx` directly |
| Fill a templated agreement | `scripts/template_fill.py` (docxtpl / Jinja) |
| Edit an existing doc | `scripts/unpack.py` → mutate XML → `scripts/pack.py` |
| Produce a tracked-changes redline | `scripts/redline.py` |
| Add comments to a passage | `scripts/comments_add.py` |
| Accept all redlines | `scripts/accept_changes.py` |
| Validate a docx before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` (mandatory final step) |

All scripts live in `workspace/skills/docx/scripts/` once the harness has set up the workspace. Invoke them via `bash`.

## Creating a new document

Pick by what you have:

- **Markdown content + a styled firm template** → `generate_from_md.py input.md out.docx template.docx`. Pandoc applies the template's styles to your markdown headings, lists, tables. Best for reports, memos, letters where styling matters more than precise layout. Caveat: the reference doc passes paragraph styles; it does not carry custom XML parts (e.g., comment threads).
- **A template with named placeholders** → `template_fill.py template.docx context.json out.docx`. docxtpl renders Jinja2 expressions inside the template. Best for engagement letters, NDAs, structured agreements.
- **Programmatic build** → write Python using `python-docx`. Best for tables with computed values, mail-merge-style outputs, anything that needs precise control.

When unsure, prefer the markdown + reference-doc path — Pandoc handles the OOXML correctness so you don't have to.

## Editing an existing document

Three-step pattern:

```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.docx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/word/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.docx
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Key files inside the unpacked tree:

- `word/document.xml` — body content (paragraphs, runs, tables)
- `word/styles.xml` — paragraph + run style definitions
- `word/numbering.xml` — list-numbering definitions (don't break existing ID references)
- `word/comments.xml` — comment thread content (created by `comments_add.py`)
- `word/header*.xml`, `word/footer*.xml` — running headers/footers
- `[Content_Types].xml` — MIME registration for every part. Edit when you add a new part type.
- `_rels/` and `word/_rels/` — relationships between parts. Edit when you reference a new image, comment, or external resource.

### Run-merging gotcha

Word writes adjacent runs (`<w:r>`) with identical formatting separately. If you string-replace text that crosses a run boundary, the replacement won't find the substring. `unpack.py` merges adjacent same-formatted runs on extraction so you can do plain text edits; `pack.py` is permissive about whatever run structure you write back.

### Smart-quote escaping

Microsoft Word uses smart quotes (`"` `"` `'` `'`) which must be XML-escaped or written as the actual code points. `unpack.py` substitutes them with XML entities for editing safety; `pack.py` reverses the substitution before zipping. Don't manually re-escape — let the scripts handle it.

### Whitespace preservation

Trailing whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements is significant. Both `unpack.py` and `pack.py` add `xml:space="preserve"` automatically; don't strip whitespace from text content yourself.

## Redlines (tracked changes)

```bash
python scripts/redline.py original.docx revised.docx redlined.docx \
  --author "Reviewer" --date "2026-04-30"
```

Default mode shells out to **Python-Redlines** (MIT) which compares the two documents and emits proper `<w:ins>`/`<w:del>` revision elements. Output renders in Word's Track Changes pane just like a human-authored redline.

### Manual mode

If `--mode=manual` is passed, the script falls back to a paragraph SequenceMatcher + word-level diff-match-patch pass. Use this when:
- Python-Redlines fails on a particular doc structure (rare).
- You want to control which paragraphs are diffed.
- The change is purely formatting (`<w:rPrChange>` runs) rather than text.

### What gets tracked

- Insertions and deletions of text
- Insertions and deletions of paragraphs (deleted-whole-paragraph case requires `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>` or you get an empty paragraph after acceptance)
- Run-property changes via `<w:rPrChange>` (formatting-only revisions)

### What doesn't get tracked

- Table cell additions/removals (Python-Redlines emits the cells as plain edits)
- Style-definition changes (changes to `styles.xml` aren't revision-trackable)
- Image swaps

## Comments

```bash
python scripts/comments_add.py document.docx comments.json
```

`comments.json` is a list of `{anchor_text, author, comment}` objects. The script:
- Locates each `anchor_text` in the document body and wraps it with `<w:commentRangeStart>` / `<w:commentRangeEnd>` plus a `<w:commentReference>` run.
- Creates or appends to `word/comments.xml` (with proper id assignment).
- Patches `[Content_Types].xml` and `word/_rels/document.xml.rels` if commenting is being added for the first time.

Anchor matching is exact-string. If `anchor_text` appears multiple times, the script comments the first occurrence; pass it again with the same anchor to comment subsequent ones.

`<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` must be siblings of the `<w:r>` runs they bracket — never nested inside a run. If you write comments by hand, follow this rule.

## Accept / reject changes

```bash
python scripts/accept_changes.py redlined.docx accepted.docx
```

Uses LibreOffice headless via a documented StarBasic macro:

```basic
Sub AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.store() End Sub
```

LibreOffice must be installed and on PATH (`soffice` binary). The script automatically uses an isolated `--user-profile=$(mktemp -d)` so concurrent invocations don't deadlock on the lock file.

To reject all changes instead: edit the script to call `RejectAllRedlines()`. To accept selectively: this isn't supported — open the doc in Word.

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring the task complete.**

```bash
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Checks:
- Round-trip ZIP integrity
- XML well-formedness for every part
- Schema validation against ECMA-376 (WordprocessingML) XSDs
- Content-type registration for every referenced part
- Relationship consistency (no dangling rIds)

Exit code 0 = valid. Non-zero exit code with line-number diagnostics = fix and re-pack.

## Common pitfalls

- **Legacy `.doc` (binary) is not supported.** Convert with `soffice --convert-to docx input.doc` first.
- **List numbering breaks after edits.** Numbering lives in `word/numbering.xml` keyed by `numId`. If you delete a list, also delete its numId reference; if you reorder, don't change IDs.
- **Headers and footers are separate parts** (`word/header1.xml`, etc.). Edits to body content don't touch them.
- **Pandoc reference-doc passes paragraph styles only.** Custom XML parts (comments, tracked changes baseline) are not carried over.
- **Don't pretty-print whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements.** Pretty-printing breaks runs that depend on exact spacing.
- **Tables: dual width specs.** Each cell needs both `columnWidths` (in `<w:tblGrid>`) and per-cell `<w:tcW w:type="dxa">`. Percentages (`pct`) render fine in Word but break in Google Docs.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- Producing PDFs from .docx: pipe through `soffice --convert-to pdf` after this skill is done.
- Signing / encryption / DRM.
- Word macros (`.docm` with VBA).



## Skill: pptx

---
name: pptx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft PowerPoint .pptx files. Covers generating decks from scratch (HTML+PptxGenJS, Marp markdown-to-slides, or python-pptx), editing existing decks in place, and validating output. For READING existing .pptx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a deck', 'create slides', 'edit slide N', 'add a chart slide'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .xlsx, or .ppt (legacy)."
---

# PPTX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .pptx, use the harness `read` tool (markitdown extracts slide text). This skill is for *writing* and *editing*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a deck from scratch (HTML/CSS) | `scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js` |
| Generate a deck from markdown | `scripts/generate_marp.sh` |
| Build slides programmatically | `python-pptx` directly |
| Edit a shape on an existing slide | `scripts/edit_shape.py` (JSON patch) |
| Add or remove a slide | unpack → edit → pack |
| QA a deck deterministically | `scripts/deterministic_qa.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Generation modalities

**HTML/CSS via PptxGenJS** (preferred for visual fidelity):
```bash
node scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js deck.json out.pptx
```
`deck.json` describes slides as a JSON tree; the script invokes PptxGenJS (and html2pptx for HTML inputs) to produce a fully-editable .pptx. Best for branded decks with gradients, custom fonts, complex shapes.

**Markdown via Marp**:
```bash
bash scripts/generate_marp.sh deck.md out.pptx
```
Best for content-heavy decks (lectures, reports) where markdown is more natural than JSON.

**Programmatic via python-pptx**:
Best when shapes are computed (e.g., one slide per data row). Requires manual EMU positioning.

## Editing existing decks

Three-step pattern, like docx:
```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.pptx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/ppt/slides/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.pptx
python scripts/validate.py output.pptx
```

For surgical shape edits without unpacking, use `edit_shape.py`:
```bash
python scripts/edit_shape.py input.pptx \
  --slide 2 --shape "Title 1" --op set_text --value "New title"
```

JSON patch ops: `set_text`, `set_position` (EMU), `set_size`, `recolor`, `delete`.

## OOXML gotchas specific to pptx

- **Use `defusedxml.minidom`, NOT `xml.etree.ElementTree`.** ElementTree corrupts presentation namespaces during round-tripping. `unpack.py` and `pack.py` use minidom; if you write your own XML manipulation, do the same.
- **EMU units everywhere.** 1 inch = 914400 EMU. Slide positions, sizes, font sizes (in pt × 100) all use derived EMU values.
- **Placeholders vs free shapes.** Placeholders inherit from slide masters; free shapes don't. Editing a placeholder's text is `<a:t>` content; editing its layout requires master-slide changes.
- **Don't pretty-print pptx XML on pack.** Whitespace-significant runs (`<a:r>`) break if reformatted. `pack.py` preserves the original whitespace.
- **Slide cloning is more than a file copy.** Use `unpack` + `pack` — manual file copies miss the rIds in `_rels/` and the `Content_Types.xml` registration.

## Deterministic QA loop

After every generation, run:

```bash
python scripts/thumbnail.py deck.pptx thumbs/   # PDF + JPEGs per slide
python scripts/deterministic_qa.py deck.pptx > qa.json
```

`deterministic_qa.py` checks:
- Shape bounding boxes don't extend past slide edges
- No two shapes overlap with > 50% area intersection
- Font sizes ≥ 11pt for body text, ≥ 18pt for titles
- All placeholders are filled (no `Click to add title` defaults)
- Bullet lists don't exceed 7 items per slide

Output is JSON listing each violation with slide number and shape id. Fix violations and re-render.

(A vision-model QA pass is intentionally out of scope for v1. Add `--use-vision` later if deterministic checks miss layout issues.)

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring done.** Schema-validates against ECMA-376 PresentationML XSDs, checks rId consistency, content-type registration.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- SmartArt creation (limited python-pptx support).
- Complex embedded charts beyond what python-pptx exposes.
- Slide transitions / animations (rarely matter for legal output).
- Vision-model layout review (deferred to v2).



## Skill: xlsx

---
name: xlsx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft Excel .xlsx files. Covers building workbooks with formulas, editing existing files, recalculating formulas, and scanning for #REF!/#DIV/0!/#VALUE! errors. For READING existing .xlsx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a model', 'create a spreadsheet', 'fill the schedule', 'recalculate'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .pptx, or .xls (legacy Excel)."
---

# XLSX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .xlsx, use the harness `read` tool (pandas extracts every sheet as a markdown table). This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *recalculating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Build a workbook from scratch | `openpyxl` directly, or `scripts/build_workbook.py` for banker conventions |
| Edit cells in an existing file | `openpyxl.load_workbook(...)` → mutate → save |
| Recalculate formulas (full fidelity) | `scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py` |
| Recalculate formulas (no LibreOffice) | `scripts/recalc_pure_python.py` |
| Scan for formula errors | `scripts/scan_errors.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Banker conventions (mandatory for financial models)

Apply these to every workbook unless the task explicitly overrides:

- **Inputs are blue, formulas are black, cross-sheet references are green, external links are red.** Use `Font(color='0000FF')` etc.
- **Negatives in parentheses, not minus signs.** Use number format `#,##0;(#,##0)`.
- **Red negatives in P&L tables.** Use `#,##0;[Red](#,##0)`.
- **Accounting format for currency.** `_-* #,##0_-;-* #,##0_-;_-* "-"_-;_-@_-` (or the localized equivalent).
- **Multiples shown as `0.0x`**, not `0.0` followed by an "x" character. Format: `0.0"x"`.
- **Underline-only on totals**, not bold-and-underline. Use `Border(bottom=Side(style='thin'))`.
- **No merged cells in input ranges.** Merged cells break formulas that reference them; reserve merging for headers and titles only.
- **Units in adjacent cells**, not in the cell with the value. `($M)` next to the value, not `"$1,234M"` as a string.

`scripts/build_workbook.py` applies these conventions automatically given a JSON spec.

## Formula authoring

- **Always emit formulas, never calculated values.** If the user wants `revenue × growth`, write `=B2*C2`, not `1234.56`. The recalc step materializes values.
- **Use named ranges** for cross-sheet inputs. `wb.defined_names["assumptions"] = DefinedName(...)`. Easier to audit.
- **Document units in adjacent cells** so the model is self-explanatory.
- **No volatile functions in hot paths.** `OFFSET`, `INDIRECT`, `NOW`, `TODAY` recalculate on every change and slow large workbooks.

## Recalculation — choose your engine

`openpyxl` writes formula *strings*; it does not evaluate them. You must recalculate before delivery, otherwise consumers will see `=B2*C2` literal text where they expect numbers (in some readers) or stale cached values (in others).

**LibreOffice path** (`recalc_libreoffice.py`) — ground truth:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Drives LibreOffice headless via the StarBasic macro `ThisComponent.calculateAll(); ThisComponent.store()`. Slow (~5–10s per workbook) but matches Excel for nearly every function. Use this when the workbook contains modern Excel features.

**Pure-Python path** (`recalc_pure_python.py`) — fast, partial:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_pure_python.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Uses `xlcalculator` to evaluate every formula in pure Python. Fast (~0.5s per workbook). Covers ~80% of common functions: arithmetic, `SUM`, `IF`, `VLOOKUP`, `INDEX`/`MATCH`, basic string/date functions.

**Does NOT support**: `XLOOKUP`, `LET`, dynamic arrays (`FILTER`, `SEQUENCE`, `UNIQUE`), `LAMBDA`, `BYROW`, `TEXTJOIN` with refs, structured table references, most modern (post-2019) Excel features.

If you used any of those, run the LibreOffice path. The pure-Python path is for CI environments without LibreOffice.

## Error scan

After every recalc, scan for formula errors:

```bash
python scripts/scan_errors.py output.xlsx > errors.json
```

Reports every cell whose computed value matches `#REF!`, `#DIV/0!`, `#VALUE!`, `#NAME?`, `#NULL!`, `#NUM!`, or `#N/A`. Output is JSONL with `{sheet, address, value}` per line.

If errors exist, fix and re-recalc. Don't ship a workbook with `#REF!`s — it's the most common reason a deliverable fails QA.

## Validation gate

`scripts/validate.py output.xlsx` schema-validates against ECMA-376 SpreadsheetML XSDs and confirms ZIP integrity, content-type registration, sheet relationships.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- **PivotTables** — `openpyxl` round-trips existing pivots but cannot create or modify them. If a task requires pivot creation, escalate (Windows-only via COM, not portable).
- **DAX measures and Power Pivot** — `openpyxl` can't write these. Same limitation.
- **VBA macros (`.xlsm`)** — out of scope for this skill. Macros require Excel runtime.
- **Conditional formatting beyond simple cell-value rules** — `openpyxl` supports basic CF; complex rules (top-N, data bars across sheets) often fail to round-trip.
- **Charts beyond the python-pptx-equivalent set** — line/bar/scatter work; combo charts and trendlines round-trip unreliably.

**EXHIBIT A**

**DESCRIPTION OF LICENSED INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY**

The Licensed IP to be delivered by Pacifica to Greenleaf pursuant to Section 3 of this Agreement shall consist of the following categories of proprietary information, in each case solely to the extent relating to Pacifica\'s proprietary extraction processes for curcumin, ashwagandha, and green tea polyphenol botanical extracts:

1\. **Extraction Process Data.** Detailed descriptions of Pacifica\'s proprietary extraction methodologies, including solvent systems (type, concentration, and ratios), temperature parameters, pressure conditions, extraction duration, and yield optimization data. This category includes flow diagrams, process narratives, and critical process parameter ranges for each botanical extract.

2\. **Analytical Methods.** Validated analytical testing methods for identity, purity, potency, and contaminant testing of botanical extracts, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) methods, spectrophotometric methods, and microbiological testing protocols. This category includes method validation reports, system suitability criteria, and reference standard specifications.

3\. **Stability Data.** Accelerated and long-term stability study data for botanical extract raw materials, including storage conditions (temperature, humidity, and light exposure parameters), shelf-life determinations, and degradation profiles. This category includes tabulated stability results, trend analyses, and recommended storage and handling conditions.

4\. **Batch Records (Redacted).** Representative batch manufacturing records demonstrating consistency of extraction processes across multiple production campaigns. Proprietary cost information, supplier pricing data, and raw material sourcing details shall be redacted from all batch records prior to delivery.

5\. **Specifications.** Current product specifications for each botanical extract, including identity tests, potency ranges, moisture content limits, heavy metals limits (lead, arsenic, cadmium, and mercury), microbial limits (total aerobic microbial count, total combined yeasts and molds, and absence of specified pathogens), and pesticide residue limits in accordance with applicable pharmacopeial standards.

6\. **Safety Data.** Toxicological summaries, certificates of analysis, and any available safety assessments relevant to the botanical extracts for purposes of FDA regulatory submissions, including summaries of acute and sub-chronic toxicity studies, genotoxicity assessments, and allergenicity evaluations where available.

The Parties\' quality assurance obligations with respect to the Products are further detailed in the QA Side Letter, which is incorporated herein by reference. In the event of any conflict between the provisions of this Agreement and the provisions of the QA Side Letter regarding quality standards, testing protocols, or related matters, the provisions of this Agreement shall control, subject to Section 15.8.

**[ARTICLE 4 --- PURCHASE COMMITMENTS AND ORDERING]{.underline}**

**Section 4.1 --- Purchase Orders**

Buyer shall submit purchase orders to Supplier in writing (including by electronic transmission) substantially in the form set forth in Exhibit D. Each purchase order shall specify, at a minimum: (a) the Product(s) ordered by Product Code; (b) the quantity ordered (in kilograms); (c) the requested delivery date; and (d) the designated delivery location. Supplier shall accept or reject each purchase order in writing within five (5) Business Days of receipt. Failure by Supplier to respond within such five (5) Business Day period shall constitute acceptance of the purchase order. Supplier may reject a purchase order only if (i) it exceeds the Binding Forecast by more than fifteen percent (15%) or (ii) the requested delivery date is less than thirty (30) days from the date of the purchase order.

**Section 4.2 --- Minimum Annual Commitment**

Buyer commits to purchase Products from Supplier with a minimum aggregate annual purchase value (the \"**Minimum Annual Commitment**\") as follows:

\(a\) **Years 1 through 3** (August 1, 2025 through July 31, 2028): Twenty-Two Million United States Dollars (\$22,000,000) per Agreement Year;

\(b\) **Years 4 through 7** (August 1, 2028 through July 31, 2032): Twenty-Eight Million United States Dollars (\$28,000,000) per Agreement Year.

For the avoidance of doubt, the total Minimum Annual Commitment over the Initial Term is One Hundred Seventy-Eight Million United States Dollars (\$178,000,000), calculated as (\$22,000,000 × 3) + (\$28,000,000 × 4) = \$66,000,000 + \$112,000,000.

For purposes of this Section 4.2, \"**Agreement Year**\" means each twelve (12)-month period commencing on August 1 and ending on the following July 31 during the Term.

If Buyer fails to meet the Minimum Annual Commitment in any Agreement Year, Buyer shall pay to Supplier a shortfall fee equal to fifteen percent (15%) of the difference between the applicable Minimum Annual Commitment and the aggregate purchase price of Products actually purchased by Buyer during such Agreement Year (the \"**Shortfall Fee**\"). The Shortfall Fee shall be due and payable within forty-five (45) days following the end of the applicable Agreement Year. Payment of the Shortfall Fee shall be Supplier\'s sole and exclusive remedy for Buyer\'s failure to meet the Minimum Annual Commitment.

**Section 4.3 --- Excess Orders**

Supplier shall use commercially reasonable efforts to fulfill purchase orders that exceed the Binding Forecast or the quantities contemplated by the rolling forecast, but Supplier shall not be obligated to accept or fulfill any such excess orders. Supplier shall notify Buyer promptly if it is unable to fulfill an excess order.

**[ARTICLE 5 --- PRICING AND PAYMENT]{.underline}**

**Section 5.1 --- Pricing**

The prices for each Product are set forth in Exhibit C (the \"**Prices**\"). Prices for Years 1 through 3 (August 1, 2025 through July 31, 2028) shall be fixed as set forth in Exhibit C. Prices for Years 4 through 7 (August 1, 2028 through July 31, 2032) shall be subject to annual adjustment in accordance with the pricing adjustment formula set forth in Exhibit C, which formula is based on a combination of an agreed raw material price index and the U.S. Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (CPI-U), as published by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. In no event shall the annual price increase for any Product exceed five percent (5%) per Agreement Year, as further specified in Exhibit C.

**Section 5.2 --- Invoicing and Payment**

Supplier shall issue an invoice to Buyer upon shipment of each order of Products. Each invoice shall reference the applicable purchase order number and shall set forth the Products shipped, quantities, unit prices, and total amount due. Payment shall be due within forty-five (45) days from the date of invoice (net 45). All payments shall be made in United States Dollars (USD) by wire transfer of immediately available funds to Supplier\'s designated bank account as specified by Supplier in writing from time to time. Buyer shall not have any right of set-off against amounts owed under this Agreement.

**Section 5.3 --- Taxes**

All Prices set forth in Exhibit C are exclusive of applicable taxes, duties, and tariffs. Buyer shall be responsible for all import duties, customs fees, and taxes imposed in connection with the importation and sale of Products within the Exclusivity Territory. Supplier shall be responsible for all export duties, fees, and taxes imposed in connection with the export of Products from Singapore. Each Party shall be responsible for its own income taxes arising from the transactions contemplated by this Agreement.

**Section 5.4 --- Late Payment**

Any amounts not paid when due under this Agreement shall bear interest from the date due until the date paid in full at a rate equal to the lesser of (a) one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month (18% per annum) and (b) the maximum rate of interest permitted by Applicable Law. The payment of interest shall not excuse or cure any default or delay in payment.

**[ARTICLE 6 --- DELIVERY AND SHIPPING]{.underline}**

**Section 6.1 --- Delivery Terms**

Unless otherwise agreed in writing, delivery of all Products shall be CIP (Carriage and Insurance Paid To) Buyer\'s designated facility in the Exclusivity Territory (Incoterms® 2020). Supplier shall arrange and pay for carriage and insurance to deliver the Products to Buyer\'s designated facility. Risk of loss and damage to the Products shall transfer from Supplier to Buyer upon delivery of the Products to the first carrier at the point of origin, in accordance with the CIP term under Incoterms® 2020. Supplier shall provide Buyer with all shipping documentation, including commercial invoices, packing lists, certificates of analysis, and bills of lading, promptly following each shipment.

**Section 6.2 --- Delivery Schedule**

Supplier shall deliver Products within the timeframes specified in each accepted purchase order. A tolerance of plus or minus five (5) Business Days from the requested delivery date shall be permitted. Delivery dates shall be calculated from the date of Supplier\'s acceptance of the purchase order. Supplier shall notify Buyer promptly if Supplier anticipates that delivery will not be made within the specified timeframe, including the reasons for any delay and the expected revised delivery date.

**Section 6.3 --- Packaging and Labeling**

All Products shall be packaged and labeled in accordance with the Specifications set forth in Exhibit B and all Applicable Laws of the Exclusivity Territory, including U.S. Food and Drug Administration (\"**FDA**\") requirements for dietary supplement ingredients under 21 C.F.R. Parts 111 and 101. Packaging shall be suitable for international ocean and/or air freight and shall protect the Products from damage, contamination, and degradation during transit and storage. Each unit shall bear a label identifying the Product name, Product Code, batch number, date of manufacture, expiration date, net weight, and country of origin.

**[ARTICLE 7 --- INSPECTION AND ACCEPTANCE]{.underline}**

**Section 7.1 --- Inspection Rights**

Buyer shall have thirty (30) days from the date of receipt of each shipment at Buyer\'s designated facility (the \"**Inspection Period**\") to inspect the Products for conformity with the Specifications, applicable regulatory requirements, and the terms of the applicable purchase order. Buyer may reject any Defective Product by delivering written notice of rejection to Supplier within the Inspection Period, specifying in reasonable detail the nature of the defect or non-conformity. Detailed testing protocols, including sampling methodologies, laboratory testing procedures, and acceptance criteria, are governed by the QA Side Letter.

**Section 7.2 --- Remedies for Defective Product**

Upon receipt of a valid notice of rejection, Supplier shall, at Supplier\'s sole option, either (a) replace the Defective Product with conforming Product at no additional charge to Buyer, with replacement shipment to be made within thirty (30) days of Supplier\'s receipt of the rejection notice, or (b) refund the purchase price paid by Buyer for such Defective Product within thirty (30) days of Supplier\'s receipt of the rejection notice. Recall obligations and cost allocation in the event of a product recall shall be governed by the QA Side Letter.

**Section 7.3 --- Deemed Acceptance**

Any Product not rejected by Buyer within the Inspection Period shall be deemed accepted by Buyer; provided, however, that such deemed acceptance shall not apply to latent defects that could not reasonably have been discovered during the Inspection Period. Claims for latent defects may be raised by Buyer in writing to Supplier within twelve (12) months of the date of delivery of the applicable Products.

**Section 13.1 --- Initial Term**

**Section 13.2 --- Renewal**

Following the expiration of the Initial Term, this Agreement shall automatically renew for two (2) successive periods of three (3) years each (each, a \"**Renewal Term**\"), unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least twelve (12) months prior to the expiration of the then-current term. **Section 13.3 --- Termination for Cause**

Either Party may terminate this Agreement by written notice to the other Party upon the occurrence of any of the following:

\(a\) **Material Breach.** The other Party commits a material breach of any provision of this Agreement, and such breach remains uncured for sixty (60) days after the non-breaching Party provides written notice to the breaching Party specifying the nature of the breach in reasonable detail;

\(b\) **Insolvency.** The other Party (i) becomes insolvent, (ii) files a voluntary petition in bankruptcy, (iii) has an involuntary petition in bankruptcy filed against it that is not dismissed within ninety (90) days, (iv) makes a general assignment for the benefit of creditors, (v) has a receiver, trustee, or liquidator appointed for all or a substantial portion of its assets, or (vi) takes or suffers any similar action under applicable insolvency or bankruptcy laws of any jurisdiction; or

\(c\) **Prolonged Force Majeure.** A Force Majeure Event affecting the other Party\'s ability to perform its material obligations under this Agreement continues for a period of more than one hundred eighty (180) consecutive days.

**Section 13.4 --- Termination by Buyer for Convenience**

Buyer may terminate this Agreement for convenience upon twelve (12) months\' prior written notice to Supplier, provided that Buyer shall pay to Supplier a termination fee equal to twenty-five percent (25%) of the remaining Minimum Annual Commitment for the balance of the then-current term (whether the Initial Term or a Renewal Term), calculated as of the effective date of termination. **Section 13.5 --- Effect of Termination**

**QUALITY ASSURANCE SIDE LETTER**

(the \"Side Letter\" or \"QA Letter\")

Dated as of August 1, 2025

This Quality Assurance Side Letter (this \"Side Letter\" or \"QA Letter\") is dated as of August 1, 2025 (the \"Effective Date\") and is entered into by and between:

**Greenleaf Nutritionals, Inc.**, a Delaware corporation (\"Greenleaf\" or \"Buyer\"), with its principal offices at 4500 Commerce Blvd., Suite 300, Charlotte, NC 28202;

and

**Pacifica Botanics Pte. Ltd.**, a Singapore private limited company (UEN 201804562R) (\"Pacifica\" or \"Supplier\"), with its registered office at 18 Raffles Quay, #22-03, Singapore 048581.

Greenleaf and Pacifica are each referred to herein individually as a \"Party\" and collectively as the \"Parties.\"

Executed simultaneously with and supplemental to the Exclusive Supply Agreement dated as of even date herewith (the \"Supply Agreement\" or \"SA\") and the IP License Agreement dated as of even date herewith (the \"IPLA\").

Prepared by Harmon & Grey LLP on behalf of Pacifica Botanics Pte. Ltd.

**[RECITALS]{.underline}**

**WHEREAS**, Greenleaf and Pacifica are entering into the Supply Agreement, pursuant to which Pacifica shall exclusively supply certain proprietary botanical extracts (including high-potency curcumin, ashwagandha, and green tea polyphenol ingredients) to Greenleaf for use in the manufacture of dietary supplements;

**WHEREAS**, the Supply Agreement has an initial term of seven (7) years commencing August 1, 2025 and ending July 31, 2032, with two (2) automatic three (3)-year renewal periods, and contemplates minimum annual purchase commitments of Twenty-Two Million United States Dollars (\$22,000,000) per year during Years 1 through 3 and Twenty-Eight Million United States Dollars (\$28,000,000) per year during Years 4 through 7, totaling One Hundred Seventy-Eight Million United States Dollars (\$178,000,000) over the initial term;

**WHEREAS**, the Parties desire to set forth the quality assurance protocols, testing standards, rejection and return procedures, recall cost allocation mechanisms, and dispute resolution procedures applicable to the Products supplied under the Supply Agreement;

**WHEREAS**, this Side Letter is intended to supplement, and be read together with, the Supply Agreement and the IPLA as part of the integrated transaction documents executed by the Parties on or about the date hereof;

**WHEREAS**, capitalized terms used but not defined herein shall have the meanings ascribed to them in the Supply Agreement; and

**NOW, THEREFORE**, in consideration of the mutual covenants set forth herein and in the Supply Agreement, and for other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the Parties agree as follows:

**[Section 1 --- Definitions]{.underline}**

**1.1** \"**Products**\" means the proprietary botanical extracts supplied by Pacifica to Greenleaf under the Supply Agreement, including curcumin extracts, ashwagandha extracts, and green tea polyphenol extracts, together with any additional botanical extract products that may be added to the scope of the Supply Agreement by mutual written agreement of the Parties from time to time.

**1.2** \"**Specifications**\" means the product specifications set forth in Exhibit A to this Side Letter, as may be updated from time to time by mutual written agreement of the Parties in accordance with Section 4.

**1.3** \"**Batch**\" means a discrete production lot of a Product identified by a unique lot number assigned by Pacifica in accordance with its internal batch numbering system and current Good Manufacturing Practices (cGMP) requirements.

**1.4** \"**Certificate of Analysis**\" or \"**COA**\" means the document issued by Pacifica for each Batch certifying the results of testing performed by Pacifica\'s internal quality laboratory in accordance with the Supplier Testing Protocols.

**1.5** \"**Defective Product**\" means any Product that fails to conform to the Specifications or applicable regulatory standards, including but not limited to cGMP requirements, United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) requirements, and applicable laws of the jurisdictions in which Greenleaf markets and sells its finished dietary supplement products.

**1.6** \"**Recall**\" means any voluntary or involuntary withdrawal, field correction, market withdrawal, or safety alert relating to a Product or any finished dietary supplement manufactured by Greenleaf using a Product, whether initiated by either Party, required by a governmental authority, or conducted pursuant to applicable law.

**1.7** \"**Expert**\" means an independent technical expert appointed in accordance with Section 7.1 of this Side Letter.

**1.8** \"**Supply Agreement**\" means the Exclusive Supply Agreement dated as of August 1, 2025 between Greenleaf and Pacifica.

**1.9** \"**IPLA**\" means the IP License Agreement dated as of even date herewith between Greenleaf and Pacifica.

**1.10** \"**Nonconformity**\" means any deviation from the Specifications identified through testing conducted under Section 2 or Section 3 of this Side Letter, including deviations in identity, potency, purity, heavy metals content, microbiological limits, residual solvents, or moisture content.

**1.11** All other capitalized terms used herein and not otherwise defined shall have the meanings set forth in the Supply Agreement.

**[Section 2 --- Supplier Testing Protocols]{.underline}**

**2.1** Pacifica shall perform testing on each Batch prior to shipment in accordance with the testing protocols set forth in Exhibit A (the \"Supplier Testing Protocols\"). At a minimum, such testing shall include the following: (a) identity testing by High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) or equivalent validated method; (b) potency and assay testing to confirm active compound levels within Specification tolerances; (c) heavy metals panel testing, including limits for lead, arsenic, cadmium, and mercury; (d) microbiological testing, including total aerobic plate count, total combined yeast and mold count, *Escherichia coli*, and *Salmonella* species; (e) residual solvent testing in accordance with applicable pharmacopeial guidelines; and (f) moisture content analysis.

**2.2** Pacifica shall issue a Certificate of Analysis for each Batch, which shall accompany the shipment documentation and be transmitted electronically to Greenleaf\'s designated quality assurance team no later than five (5) business days prior to the expected delivery date. Each COA shall identify the Batch by lot number, date of manufacture, date of testing, testing methodology employed, and results for each parameter tested.

**2.3** Pacifica shall retain reserve samples from each Batch for a minimum of three (3) years from the date of shipment, or such longer period as may be required by applicable law or regulation. Pacifica shall make such retained samples available to Greenleaf upon reasonable written request, at Greenleaf\'s expense for shipping and handling.

**2.4** Pacifica warrants that all testing conducted pursuant to this Section 2 shall be performed by qualified personnel in laboratories that maintain ISO 17025 accreditation or equivalent accreditation standards recognized by the relevant competent authorities. Pacifica shall provide evidence of such accreditation to Greenleaf upon request.

**2.5** Pacifica shall notify Greenleaf in writing within forty-eight (48) hours of becoming aware of any deviation, out-of-specification result, or laboratory error affecting a Batch that has been shipped or is in transit to Greenleaf\'s designated receiving facility. Such notification shall include the lot number(s) affected, the nature of the deviation, and Pacifica\'s preliminary assessment of the potential impact on Product quality and safety.

**[Section 3 --- Buyer Verification Testing]{.underline}**

**3.1** Greenleaf shall have the right, but not the obligation, to perform incoming verification testing on any Batch within thirty (30) calendar days of receipt at Greenleaf\'s designated receiving facility (the \"Buyer Verification Period\"). Greenleaf\'s decision not to perform verification testing on any particular Batch shall not be construed as acceptance of such Batch or as a waiver of any rights under this Side Letter or the Supply Agreement.

**3.2** Greenleaf\'s verification testing may include, at Greenleaf\'s sole discretion, any or all of the tests described in Section 2.1, as well as additional tests reasonably related to Greenleaf\'s finished product quality requirements, regulatory compliance obligations, and customer specifications.

**3.3** If Greenleaf\'s verification testing reveals a Nonconformity, Greenleaf shall promptly notify Pacifica in writing, providing: (a) a description of the Nonconformity; (b) copies or summaries of the applicable test results; and (c) the lot number(s) of the affected Batch(es).

**3.4** In the event of a discrepancy between Pacifica\'s COA results and Greenleaf\'s verification testing results for the same Batch, the Parties shall engage in good-faith discussions to resolve the discrepancy, including, if appropriate, the exchange of retained samples and testing data. If the Parties are unable to resolve the discrepancy within fifteen (15) business days of Greenleaf\'s notification, either Party may submit the dispute for expert determination in accordance with Section 7.1.

**3.5** Greenleaf\'s failure to perform verification testing or to notify Pacifica of a Nonconformity within the Buyer Verification Period shall not constitute acceptance of the Batch or a waiver of Greenleaf\'s right to assert claims for Defective Product discovered thereafter, including claims relating to latent defects not reasonably discoverable through standard incoming testing procedures.

**[Section 4 --- Specifications and Amendments]{.underline}**

**4.1** The current Specifications for each Product category are set forth in Exhibit A to this Side Letter. Specifications may be amended only by mutual written agreement of the Parties, which agreement shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed by either Party.

**4.2** If a change in applicable law, regulation, or guidance (including, without limitation, FDA guidance documents, United States Pharmacopeia (USP) monographs, or equivalent standards in jurisdictions where Greenleaf distributes finished products) requires modification of the Specifications, the Parties shall negotiate in good faith to revise the Specifications to comply with such requirement within sixty (60) calendar days of written notice from either Party identifying the regulatory change and the required modifications.

**4.3** Pacifica shall not change its manufacturing process, extraction methodology, raw material sources, or facility locations in any manner that could reasonably be expected to affect the quality, composition, potency, safety, or regulatory status of the Products without providing Greenleaf at least thirty (30) days\' prior written notice and obtaining Greenleaf\'s written approval, which approval shall not be unreasonably withheld. Pacifica\'s written notice shall include a detailed description of the proposed change, the rationale therefor, and a technical assessment of the potential impact on Product quality.

**4.4** Greenleaf reserves the right to audit Pacifica\'s manufacturing and testing facilities upon reasonable prior written notice of not less than fifteen (15) business days, no more than twice per calendar year, during normal business hours. In the event of a quality event, including but not limited to discovery of Defective Product, a Recall, or a regulatory inquiry relating to a Product, Greenleaf may conduct additional audits as reasonably necessary to investigate and resolve the quality event, upon not less than five (5) business days\' notice. Pacifica shall cooperate fully with any such audit and shall provide Greenleaf\'s auditors with reasonable access to facilities, records, and personnel.

**[Section 5 --- Rejection and Return Procedures]{.underline}**

**5.1** Greenleaf may reject any Batch that constitutes Defective Product by providing written notice to Pacifica within the Buyer Verification Period (thirty (30) calendar days of receipt) for patent defects, or within thirty (30) calendar days of discovery for latent defects that are not reasonably discoverable through standard incoming verification testing.

**5.2** Upon receipt of a valid rejection notice, Pacifica shall, at Greenleaf\'s election: (a) replace the rejected Batch with conforming Product at Pacifica\'s sole cost and expense (including all shipping, freight, duties, and insurance costs) within thirty (30) calendar days of receipt of the rejection notice; or (b) issue a full credit or refund for the rejected Batch, calculated at the invoice price for such Batch, within fifteen (15) business days of receipt of the rejection notice.

**5.3** Rejected Product shall be segregated and held by Greenleaf at Pacifica\'s risk and expense pending Pacifica\'s written return instructions. If Pacifica does not provide return instructions within thirty (30) calendar days of the rejection notice, Greenleaf may dispose of the rejected Product in accordance with applicable law and Pacifica shall bear the reasonable cost of such disposal.

**5.4** If Pacifica disputes a rejection, Pacifica shall notify Greenleaf in writing within ten (10) business days of receipt of the rejection notice, setting forth in reasonable detail the basis for its dispute. The disputed rejection shall then be submitted for resolution in accordance with Section 7 (Dispute Resolution). Pending resolution of the dispute, Greenleaf shall not be required to pay for the disputed Batch, and Greenleaf shall segregate and preserve the rejected Product and representative samples thereof.

**5.5** Acceptance of any Batch, whether by express acknowledgment, use, or passage of time, shall not limit or prejudice Greenleaf\'s right to assert warranty claims, indemnification claims, or other remedies under this Side Letter or the Supply Agreement for latent defects or Nonconformities not reasonably discoverable through incoming verification testing at the time of receipt.

**[Section 6 --- Recall Cost Allocation]{.underline}**

**6.1** In the event of a Recall attributable to a Defective Product supplied by Pacifica, Pacifica shall bear all direct costs and expenses arising from or related to the Recall, including but not limited to: (a) costs of notification to customers, distributors, retailers, and regulatory authorities; (b) costs of retrieving, returning, or destroying affected product (whether in raw material, work-in-process, or finished product form); (c) costs of replacement product, including expedited manufacturing and shipping; (d) costs of regulatory submissions, communications, and compliance activities; (e) costs of third-party testing, investigation, and root cause analysis; and (f) reasonable internal administrative costs incurred by Greenleaf in managing the Recall.

**6.2** In the event of a Recall caused by factors attributable solely to Greenleaf (including but not limited to manufacturing defects introduced during Greenleaf\'s processing, improper storage or handling by Greenleaf after delivery, or labeling errors attributable to Greenleaf), Greenleaf shall bear all costs of the nature described in Section 6.1.

**6.3** In the event of a Recall caused by a combination of factors attributable to both Parties, the Parties shall allocate costs in proportion to their respective contributions to the cause of the Recall, as determined by mutual agreement of the Parties or, if the Parties cannot agree within thirty (30) calendar days following completion of the root cause investigation, as determined in accordance with Section 7.

**6.4** Each Party shall cooperate fully and in good faith with the other in connection with any Recall, including providing prompt access to records, samples, and personnel, and shall use commercially reasonable efforts to mitigate Recall costs and minimize harm to consumers.

**6.5** Pacifica shall maintain product liability insurance with a reputable international insurer, with a per-occurrence limit of not less than Ten Million United States Dollars (\$10,000,000) and an aggregate annual limit of not less than Twenty Million United States Dollars (\$20,000,000), naming Greenleaf as an additional insured. Pacifica shall provide Greenleaf with certificates of insurance evidencing such coverage upon execution of this Side Letter and annually thereafter, and shall provide not less than thirty (30) days\' prior written notice to Greenleaf of any material change in, cancellation of, or failure to renew such coverage.

**6.6** Nothing in this Section 6 shall limit or modify Greenleaf\'s rights to consequential damages, indemnification, or other remedies as provided in the Supply Agreement, except to the extent expressly limited by the dispute resolution provisions of Article 14 of the Supply Agreement.

**[Section 7 --- Dispute Resolution]{.underline}**

**7.1 Expert Determination**

Any dispute arising under this Side Letter relating to product quality, Nonconformity, testing methodology, Specifications compliance, or recall obligations shall be resolved exclusively by expert determination administered by the Singapore International Arbitration Centre (\"SIAC\") under its Rules for the Appointment of Experts then in effect. The Expert shall be an independent person with relevant technical expertise in botanical extract manufacturing, analytical chemistry, food safety, or dietary supplement quality assurance, as appropriate to the nature of the dispute. The Expert shall not have any current or prior relationship with either Party or their respective affiliates, counsel, or testing laboratories. The Expert\'s determination shall be rendered within sixty (60) calendar days of appointment, unless the Parties mutually agree to an extension. Each Party shall bear its own costs (including attorneys\' fees and internal costs) in connection with the expert determination and shall share equally the Expert\'s fees and expenses and SIAC\'s administrative fees. The Expert\'s determination shall be final and binding on the Parties, subject to Section 7.2.

**7.2 Escalation to Arbitration**

If the dispute involves an amount in excess of Five Million United States Dollars (\$5,000,000), or if the expert determination rendered pursuant to Section 7.1 is challenged by either Party, the dispute shall be escalated to binding arbitration administered by the Singapore International Arbitration Centre under its Arbitration Rules then in effect, with the seat of arbitration in Singapore and the tribunal consisting of three (3) arbitrators. The language of the arbitration shall be English. The three arbitrators shall be appointed in accordance with the SIAC Arbitration Rules: each Party shall nominate one (1) arbitrator within thirty (30) days of the commencement of arbitration, and the two Party-nominated arbitrators shall select the presiding arbitrator within fifteen (15) days of their appointment; failing agreement on the presiding arbitrator within such period, the presiding arbitrator shall be appointed by SIAC. The arbitral tribunal shall have the authority to award any remedy or relief available under applicable law, including injunctive relief, specific performance, and damages.

**7.3 Governing Law**

The governing law for disputes under this Side Letter shall be the laws of the Republic of Singapore.

**[Section 8 --- Relationship to Supply Agreement]{.underline}**

**8.1** This Side Letter supplements and forms part of the Supply Agreement. In the event of any conflict or inconsistency between the terms of this Side Letter and the terms of the Supply Agreement, the Supply Agreement shall control; *provided, however*, that with respect to the subject matters specifically addressed in this Side Letter --- including testing protocols, rejection and return procedures, recall cost allocation, and the dispute resolution mechanism set forth in Section 7 --- this Side Letter shall control. The Parties acknowledge that this Side Letter, together with the Supply Agreement and the IPLA, forms part of an integrated suite of transaction documents and shall be construed accordingly.

**8.2** The rights and remedies of the Parties under this Side Letter are cumulative and in addition to the rights and remedies under the Supply Agreement and the IPLA, except as expressly limited herein.

**8.3** All notices, requests, demands, and other communications under this Side Letter shall be given in accordance with the notice provisions set forth in Article 15 of the Supply Agreement.

**[Section 9 --- General Provisions]{.underline}**

**9.1** **Amendment.** This Side Letter may be amended, modified, or supplemented only by a written instrument executed by duly authorized representatives of both Parties.

**9.2** **Waiver.** No waiver of any provision of this Side Letter shall be effective unless set forth in a written instrument signed by the Party granting such waiver. No failure or delay by either Party in exercising any right, power, or remedy under this Side Letter shall operate as a waiver thereof, nor shall any single or partial exercise of any such right, power, or remedy preclude any other or further exercise thereof.

**9.3** **Severability.** If any provision of this Side Letter is held by a court or tribunal of competent jurisdiction to be invalid, illegal, or unenforceable, the remaining provisions shall remain in full force and effect, and the Parties shall negotiate in good faith to replace the invalid provision with a valid provision that achieves, to the extent possible, the economic, business, and other purposes of the invalid provision.

**9.4** **Counterparts.** This Side Letter may be executed in one or more counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original and all of which together shall constitute one and the same instrument. Delivery of an executed counterpart by facsimile or electronic transmission (including PDF) shall be equally effective as delivery of a manually executed counterpart, and electronic signatures shall be deemed original signatures for all purposes.

**9.5** **Headings.** Section headings used in this Side Letter are for convenience of reference only and shall not affect the interpretation or construction of any provision hereof.

**9.6** **Entire Agreement.** This Side Letter, together with the Supply Agreement and the IPLA (including all exhibits, schedules, and annexes thereto), constitutes the entire agreement of the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior negotiations, representations, warranties, commitments, offers, and agreements, whether written or oral, relating to such subject matter.

**[I. Purpose and Scope]{.underline}**

This memorandum summarizes the rationale behind the dispute resolution provisions drafted by Harmon & Grey LLP across the three principal transaction documents comprising the Greenleaf Nutritionals / Pacifica Botanics strategic supply relationship: (i) the Exclusive Supply Agreement (the \"**SA**\"), (ii) the Quality Assurance Side Letter (the \"**QA Letter**\"), and (iii) the IP License Agreement (the \"**IPLA**\"), each to be entered into between Pacifica Botanics Pte. Ltd. (\"**Pacifica**\") and Greenleaf Nutritionals, Inc. (\"**Greenleaf**\").

Harmon & Grey drafted the initial arbitration and dispute resolution clauses across all three documents at Pacifica\'s instruction. This memorandum is being shared with Greenleaf\'s counsel, Whitfield & Crane LLP, to facilitate their review and to expedite negotiation of any proposed revisions in advance of the anticipated signing date of **July 18, 2025**, and the Target Effective Date of **August 1, 2025**. We respectfully request that Whitfield & Crane provide any comments on the dispute resolution provisions no later than July 11, 2025, to allow sufficient time for resolution before signing.

By way of commercial context, the transaction establishes a seven-year exclusive supply relationship with minimum annual purchase commitments of USD \$22,000,000 during Years 1 through 3 and USD \$28,000,000 during Years 4 through 7, for aggregate minimum commitments of approximately USD \$178,000,000 over the initial term. The SA further provides for two automatic three-year renewal periods, subject to certain conditions. Given the scale and duration of this relationship, the dispute resolution architecture was crafted with particular care to ensure that any disagreements between the parties are resolved efficiently, predictably, and with access to decision-makers possessing the appropriate institutional and subject-matter expertise.

**[II. Overview of the Dispute Resolution Framework]{.underline}**

The transaction documents employ a three-tier dispute resolution structure, with each agreement directing disputes to the institution and forum best suited to its subject matter. This architecture was designed deliberately to match the nature of the obligations under each agreement with the institutional competence and procedural tools most likely to produce efficient and well-reasoned outcomes.

**A. Supply Agreement --- Article 14 (ICC Arbitration)**

The SA, which constitutes the core commercial framework governing pricing, delivery schedules, exclusivity obligations, and termination rights, provides for arbitration under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce (\"**ICC**\"). The ICC\'s established reputation for administering complex international commercial disputes, its experienced Secretariat, and its global reach make it the natural institution for the principal commercial agreement in this transaction.

Pursuant to SA Section 14.1, any dispute arising out of or relating to the SA shall be finally resolved by arbitration administered by the ICC. The juridical seat of arbitration is Singapore (Section 14.2), selected because Pacifica is incorporated in Singapore and the curial law of Singapore provides a well-developed, pro-arbitration framework for international arbitration. The venue of hearings shall be London, England, given its position as a leading centre for international arbitration and its geographic midpoint between the parties\' principal operations. London was chosen as a neutral, convenient hearing location offering world-class arbitration facilities and infrastructure, and the distinction between the seat and the hearing location is a routine feature of modern international arbitration practice.

Section 14.3 provides that the arbitration shall be conducted by a sole arbitrator, with either party retaining the right to request a panel of three arbitrators within fifteen (15) days of service of the Request for Arbitration. This approach offers the parties flexibility, allowing straightforward disputes to proceed efficiently before a sole arbitrator while preserving the option for a full panel in matters of greater complexity or value.

The governing law of the SA is the law of the State of New York, United States, without regard to conflict-of-laws principles (Section 14.4). This reflects the fact that Greenleaf\'s commercial operations are centered in the United States and New York law is widely understood and applied in international commercial disputes of this nature.

**B. Quality Assurance Side Letter --- Section 7 (SIAC Expert Determination / Arbitration)**

The QA Letter, which governs product specifications, testing methodologies, acceptance criteria, and rejection rights, employs a specialized two-step dispute resolution mechanism under the auspices of the Singapore International Arbitration Centre (\"**SIAC**\").

In the first instance, technical quality disputes --- including disagreements regarding testing methodology, conformance to product specifications, and the exercise of rejection rights --- are submitted to expert determination under the SIAC Rules for the Appointment of Experts (Section 7.1). This mechanism was designed to ensure that disputes of a predominantly technical or scientific character are resolved by individuals with the relevant subject-matter expertise, rather than by generalist arbitrators. Expert determination offers a streamlined, cost-effective resolution pathway appropriate for the majority of quality-related disagreements likely to arise in the ordinary course of performance.

Where a dispute exceeds USD \$5,000,000 in value or where the expert determination is challenged, the matter escalates to full arbitration administered by SIAC (Section 7.2). The USD \$5,000,000 threshold was calibrated to distinguish routine quality disagreements --- which should be resolved quickly and inexpensively --- from material disputes warranting the procedural protections and comprehensive adjudication of a full arbitral proceeding.

SIAC was chosen as the administering institution for the QA Letter given its expertise in Asia-Pacific commercial disputes and the fact that its dedicated expert determination rules are well-suited to the technical quality issues likely to arise in connection with Pacifica\'s Singapore-based manufacturing and quality control operations.

The governing law of the QA Letter is Singapore law (Section 7.3), selected because the QA Letter primarily concerns Pacifica\'s manufacturing and testing operations conducted in Singapore, and Singapore law provides the most natural legal framework for interpreting obligations performed within the jurisdiction.

**C. IP License Agreement --- Section 9 (LCIA Arbitration)**

The IPLA, which governs the cross-licensing of certain intellectual property rights, regulatory data packages, and proprietary formulations, provides for arbitration under the Rules of the London Court of International Arbitration (\"**LCIA**\"). The LCIA was selected because the IPLA concerns intellectual property rights and regulatory data, which the parties considered best suited to London-seated arbitration under English law.

English law governs the IPLA (Section 9.2), reflecting the well-developed framework for IP licensing under English law and its widespread use in international intellectual property transactions. The arbitration shall be conducted by a sole arbitrator, in the English language.

Section 9.3 contains a consolidation provision intended as a practical efficiency measure to avoid duplicative proceedings. Where claims under the IPLA also relate to obligations arising under the SA, such claims shall be heard jointly in a single arbitration proceeding conducted under IPLA Section 9 and administered by the LCIA. This ensures that overlapping disputes touching both IP rights and supply obligations are resolved in a unified proceeding, avoiding the risk of inconsistent decisions and unnecessary costs.

**[III. Detailed Rationale for Key Provisions in Article 14 of the Supply Agreement]{.underline}**

**A. Seat and Venue Selection (Section 14.2)**

Singapore was selected as the juridical seat of arbitration under the SA for several compelling reasons. First, Singapore is a signatory to the 1958 New York Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards, ensuring that any award rendered in a Singapore-seated arbitration is enforceable in over 170 contracting states, including the United States. Second, Singapore\'s International Arbitration Act (Cap. 143A), which incorporates the UNCITRAL Model Law on International Commercial Arbitration, provides a modern, pro-arbitration curial framework that is internationally recognized and trusted. Third, Pacifica is domiciled in Singapore, and situating the juridical seat in Singapore facilitates enforcement of any award against Pacifica\'s assets if necessary, thereby providing Greenleaf with meaningful recourse. Fourth, Singapore\'s courts have a strong and well-documented track record of minimal intervention in arbitral proceedings, consistent with international best practice.

London was selected as the physical hearing location on practical grounds. London is approximately equidistant between Charlotte, North Carolina --- where Greenleaf\'s principal offices are located --- and Singapore, providing a geographically convenient meeting point. London also offers world-class arbitration hearing facilities at institutions including the International Dispute Resolution Centre, and the availability of experienced international arbitrators based in or regularly traveling through London is a significant practical advantage. Additionally, both parties\' counsel maintain London-based resources and personnel, reducing logistical friction.

The distinction between the juridical seat and the physical venue of hearings is well-established in international arbitration practice and should not give rise to any procedural difficulty. The seat determines the curial law and the supervisory jurisdiction for any post-award challenge, while the hearing location is simply the place where oral hearings are convened as a matter of practical convenience. This arrangement is commonplace in major international arbitrations.

**B. Finality and Limited Appeal Right (Section 14.6)**

Section 14.6 was drafted to ensure the finality of arbitral awards while preserving a carefully defined \"safety valve\" allowing appeal on questions of law. This provision was modeled on the approach under the English Arbitration Act 1996, specifically Section 69, which permits parties to agree to appeal arbitral awards on questions of law to the courts. We believe this provision strikes an appropriate balance between finality and the ability to correct material errors of law. The availability of appellate review on points of law provides both parties with assurance that the tribunal\'s legal reasoning will be subject to judicial oversight, thereby enhancing the legitimacy and reliability of the arbitral process.

Section 14.6 provides that any appeal on a question of law arising out of an award shall be brought before the High Court of Singapore, sitting in its capacity as the supervisory court at the juridical seat. This channels post-award review to a single, predictable forum, avoiding the prospect of parallel or competing challenge proceedings in multiple jurisdictions.

Pacifica\'s recent experience with post-award challenges underscores the importance of this provision. In 2023, Pacifica prevailed in an ICC arbitration seated in Hong Kong against a Korean buyer, involving claims valued at approximately USD \$8,200,000 relating to rejected shipments. Following the award, the losing party challenged enforcement in Korean courts, citing alleged defects in the arbitration clause. Although the challenge was ultimately unsuccessful, it delayed enforcement by approximately fourteen months, imposing significant cost and commercial disruption on Pacifica. The appeal mechanism in Section 14.6 was drafted with the benefit of that experience, and is intended to ensure that any post-award review is channeled through a single, clearly defined appellate procedure before the courts at the seat, rather than arising unpredictably during enforcement proceedings abroad.

**C. Discovery Limitations (Section 14.5)**

Section 14.5 provides that no pre-hearing discovery, disclosure, or document production of any kind shall be ordered by the tribunal or requested by the parties. This provision was drafted deliberately and reflects Pacifica\'s strongly held view --- shared, we understand, by Greenleaf --- that international commercial arbitration is not intended to replicate the broad discovery mechanisms available in U.S. federal or state courts.

The international consensus, reflected in instruments such as the IBA Rules on the Taking of Evidence in International Arbitration, is that document production in international arbitration should be limited and targeted, rather than open-ended. Section 14.5 is consistent with this prevailing international norm and ensures that the arbitration proceeds on the basis of documentary evidence and witness testimony presented by the parties, without the burden, expense, and delay associated with extensive pre-hearing discovery.

The provision also serves an important commercial purpose. Pacifica\'s proprietary manufacturing processes, botanical extraction methodologies, and trade secrets are the cornerstone of its competitive position. Broad, U.S.-style discovery would expose Pacifica to the risk of fishing expeditions targeting these proprietary assets, with no guarantee that confidentiality orders would be sufficient to prevent disclosure to competitors. Section 14.5 eliminates this risk at the outset.

Critically, each party retains the right to present documentary evidence and witness testimony at the hearing. The restriction applies solely to compelled pre-hearing production; voluntary disclosure and submission of documentary evidence in support of a party\'s case are unaffected. This ensures that due process is preserved while eliminating the procedural burden that broad discovery would impose.

**D. Damages Limitations (Section 14.11)**

Section 14.11 contains a mutual waiver of punitive, exemplary, and consequential damages. This exclusion is standard in international supply agreements of this nature and ensures that the arbitral tribunal focuses on compensating actual, demonstrated losses rather than speculative or indirect harm. Both parties benefit from certainty as to the scope of potential liability, and the mutual character of the waiver ensures that neither party bears disproportionate exposure.

**E. Limitation Period (Section 14.9)**

Section 14.9 establishes a one-year limitation period for the commencement of arbitral proceedings. Given the nature of the supply relationship --- involving recurring shipments, ongoing quality monitoring, and annual pricing adjustments --- a one-year limitation period ensures that disputes are raised and resolved promptly, preventing the accumulation of stale claims that would be difficult to adjudicate fairly.

**F. Interim Relief (Section 14.7)**

Section 14.7 preserves each party\'s right to seek injunctive or other equitable relief from any court of competent jurisdiction, notwithstanding the arbitration agreement. This carve-out is standard and necessary to ensure that parties can protect their rights in urgent circumstances --- for example, misuse of licensed intellectual property or a supply disruption threatening public health or regulatory compliance.

**G. Cost Allocation (Section 14.8)**

Section 14.8 provides that the costs of arbitration, including reasonable legal fees, shall be borne by the unsuccessful party. This loser-pays fee-shifting mechanism is intended to discourage frivolous claims and encourage settlement.

**H. Confidentiality (Section 14.10)**

Section 14.10 requires that all arbitral proceedings, submissions, evidence, and awards remain confidential, subject to a limited carve-out for disclosures required by applicable law or regulation. This provision is standard and protects both parties\' proprietary and commercially sensitive information.

**[IV. Cross-Agreement Coordination]{.underline}**

The three transaction documents each contain bespoke dispute resolution provisions tailored to the subject matter of the respective agreement. This approach reflects the distinct character of the obligations in each instrument: the SA\'s commercial and supply terms, the QA Letter\'s technical quality requirements, and the IPLA\'s intellectual property and regulatory data provisions.

The parties have addressed the potential for overlap or inconsistency through the integration and merger clause in SA Section 15.8, which establishes a clear hierarchy among the transaction documents. In the event of any conflict between the terms of the SA and the terms of the QA Letter or IPLA, the SA shall control. This integration clause provides a clear and enforceable hierarchy, ensuring that the SA\'s terms take precedence in the event of any ambiguity or inconsistency across the document suite.

We recognize that the parties may wish to discuss whether to harmonize certain procedural aspects of the dispute resolution mechanisms --- for example, aligning timelines for notice or agreeing on common procedural calendars where disputes under multiple agreements arise concurrently. We are open to such discussions. However, we are of the view that the current structure, which reflects commercial and legal realities specific to each agreement, is workable and does not require fundamental restructuring. The distinct institutions and governing laws selected for each agreement ensure that each type of dispute is resolved by the decision-maker and under the legal framework best suited to the issues at hand.

**[V. Pacifica\'s Arbitration Experience and Lessons Learned]{.underline}**

As noted above, Pacifica\'s 2023 ICC arbitration provides important context for the current dispute resolution architecture. In that matter, Pacifica brought claims valued at approximately USD \$8,200,000 against a Korean buyer arising from the wrongful rejection of multiple shipments of botanical extract. The arbitration was seated in Hong Kong and conducted under ICC Rules. Pacifica obtained a favorable award.

However, the losing party challenged enforcement in the Korean courts, contending that the arbitration clause was \"pathological\" due to certain alleged ambiguities in the clause\'s drafting. Although the Korean court ultimately rejected the challenge and permitted enforcement, the proceedings delayed recovery by approximately fourteen months and imposed substantial additional legal costs on Pacifica.

The arbitration provisions in the current transaction documents were drafted with the benefit of that experience. Our objective was to minimize the risk of post-award challenge and enforcement delay by ensuring that each arbitration clause is clear, specific, and enforceable, with well-defined institutional rules, seat designations, and procedural parameters. We are confident that the current provisions achieve this objective.

**[VI. Conclusion and Recommendation]{.underline}**

Harmon & Grey is confident that the dispute resolution framework across the SA, QA Letter, and IPLA is comprehensive, balanced, and enforceable. The three-tier structure matches each agreement\'s subject matter with the institution, governing law, and procedural mechanism best suited to resolve disputes efficiently and predictably.

We recommend that Pacifica share this memorandum with Greenleaf\'s counsel at Whitfield & Crane LLP to facilitate efficient negotiation of any proposed revisions to the dispute resolution provisions. Harmon & Grey remains available to discuss any questions from Pacifica\'s management team or from Greenleaf\'s counsel. We are willing to consider reasonable adjustments to the current provisions, while noting that the core architecture reflects Pacifica\'s strong preference for Singapore-connected dispute resolution and was carefully designed in light of Pacifica\'s prior arbitration experience.

Respectfully submitted,

**Darren Loh Wei Keat** Partner, Harmon & Grey LLP 9 Battery Road, #18-01, Singapore 049910 Tel: +65 6221 8847

Email: darren.loh@harmongreyllp.com

Sharing of this memorandum does not create an attorney-client relationship between Harmon & Grey LLP and Greenleaf Nutritionals, Inc. or Whitfield & Crane LLP.
